LinearAlgebra.linear algebra study?: Linear algebra is the study of vector spaces and their homomorphisms.
LinearAlgebra.a vector space?: If K is a field, then a vector space over k is an additive abelian group V equipped with a scalar multiplication
LinearAlgebra.axioms for a vector space?: V is called a vector space (over the field K) if the following axioms hold for any vectors u, v, w //in V:      [A1] (u + v) + w = u + (v + w)      [A2] There is a vector in V, denoted by 0 and called the zero vector, such that, for any u //in V, u + 0 = 0 + u = 0.       [A3] For each u //in V, there is a vector in V, denoted by -u, and called the negative of u, such that u + (-u) = (-u) + u = 0.       [A4] u + v = v + u       [M1] k(u + v) = ku + kv, for any scalar k //in K.       [M2](a + b)u = au + bu, for any scalars a, b //in K.       [M3](ab)u = a(bu), for any scalars a, b //in K.       [M4] 1u = u for the unit scalar 1 //in K.
LinearAlgebra.k0, k //in K, 0 //in V, where V is a ve: For any scalar k //in K and 0 //in V, k0 = 0
LinearAlgebra.0u, u //in V, 0 //in K, where V is a ve: For 0 //in K and any vector u //in V, 0u = 0.
LinearAlgebra.ku = 0 in a vector space imply about k a: If ku = 0, where k //in K and u //in V, then k = 0 or u = 0.
LinearAlgebra.(-k)u?: For any k //in K and any u //in V, (-k)u = k(-u) = -ku
LinearAlgebra.k(-u)?: For any k //in K and any u //in V, k(-u) = (-k)u = -ku
LinearAlgebra.scalar multiplication?: Scalar multiplication assigns to any u //in V, k //in K, the product ku //in V
LinearAlgebra.vectors in a vector space?: The vectors in a vector space are the elements of the vector space.
LinearAlgebra.scalars in a vector space?: The scalars in a vector space are the elements of the field.
LinearAlgebra.it mean for v to be a linear combination: Let V be a vector space over a field K. A vector v in V is a linear combination of vectors u_1, u_2, ..., u_m in V if there exist scalars a_1, a_2, ..., a_m in K such that v = a_1u_1 + a_2u_2 + ... + a_mu_m.
v is a linear combination of u_1, ..., u_m if there is a solution to the vector equation v = x_1u_1 + ... + x_mu_m where x_1, ..., x_m are unknown scalars.
LinearAlgebra.vectors to spanning V?: Vectors u_1, ..., u_m are said to span V if every v //in V can be written as a linear combination of the vectors u_1, ..., u_m.
LinearAlgebra.a spanning set of V?: Vectors u_1, ..., u_m are said to form a spanning set of V if every v in V is a linear combination of the vectors u_1, ..., u_m.
LinearAlgebra.u_1, ..., u_m spanning V imply about w,: Suppose u_1, ..., u_m span V. Then, for any vector w, the set w, u_1, ..., u_m also spans V.
LinearAlgebra.u_1, ..., u_m if u_k is a linear combin: Suppose u_1, ..., u_m span V and suppose some u_k is a linear combination of some of the other u's. Then the u's without u_k also span V.
LinearAlgebra.span (u_1, ..., u_m) if u_k = 0 for som: Suppose u_1, ..., u_m span V and suppose u_k = 0 for some k. Then the u's without u_k also span V.
LinearAlgebra.span(u_1, ..., u_m), where u_1, ..., u_: The collection of all linear combinations of u_1, ..., u_m is called the linear span of u_1, ..., u_m, denoted span(u_1, ..., u_m).
LinearAlgebra.the collection of linear combinations o: The collection of all linear combinations of u_1, ..., u_m is called the linear span of u_1, ..., u_m
LinearAlgebra.the linear span of u_1, ..., u_m?: The collection of all linear combinations of u_1, ..., u_m is called the linear span of u_1, ..., u_m
LinearAlgebra.Is the linear span of a set of vectors a: Span(u_1, ..., u_m) is a subspace of a vector space.       Clearly the zero vector belongs to span (u_1, ..., u_m) since 0 = 0u_1 + ... + 0u_m.       Furthermore, suppose v and v' belong to span(u_1, ..., u_m). Then v + v' //in span(u_1, ..., u_m) and kv //in span(u_1, ..., u_m).
LinearAlgebra.a subspace of a vector space?: Let V be a vector space over a field K and let W be a subset of V. Then W is a subspace of V if W is itself a vector space over K with respect to the operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication on V.
LinearAlgebra.the test to see if a subset of a vector: Suppose W is a subset of a vector space V. Then W is a subspace of V if the following two conditions hold:       (a) The zero vector belongs to W.       (b) For every u, v //in W, a, b //in K, the linear combination au + bv //in W.
LinearAlgebra.a proper subspace of a vector space?: A subspace U \subseteq V with U \ne V is called a proper subspace of V.
LinearAlgebra.If U and W are subspaces of a vector spa: The intersection of any number of subspaces of a vector space V is a subspace of V.
LinearAlgebra.the intersection of subspaces of a vect: The intersection of any number of subspaces of a vector space V is a subspace of V.
LinearAlgebra.a subspace of a vector space that conta: Let S be a subset of a vector space V. If W is a subspace of V that contains S, then span(S) \subseteq W.
LinearAlgebra.the span of a set of vectors?: Let S be a subset of a vector space V. Then span(S) is a subspace of V that contains S.
LinearAlgebra.the row space of a matrix?: Let A be an arbitrary m \times n matrix over a field K. The rows of A may be viewed as vectors in K^n; hence they span a subspace of K^n called the row space of A denoted by RowSp(A). That is RowSp(A) = span(R_1, ..., R_m)
LinearAlgebra.the column space of a matrix?: Let A be an arbitrary m \times n matrix over a field K. The columns of A may be viewed as vectors in K<sup>m</sup>; hence they span a subspace of K<sup>m</sup> called the column space of A and denoted by ColSp(A).
LinearAlgebra.matrices being row equivalent?: Matrices A and B are row equivalent, written A ~ B, if B can be obtained from A by a sequence of elementary row operations. These elementary row operations include:       Interchange R_i and R_k.       Replace R_i by kR_i.       Replace R_k by kR_i + R_k
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the row spaces: Row equivalent matrices have the same row space.
LinearAlgebra.an echilon matrix?: An echilon matrix is a matrix such that for each succeeding row, the leading nonzero term is to the right of the previous row.
LinearAlgebra.row equivalent echelon matrices?: Two equivalent echilon matrices will have the same number of nonzero rows and will have pivots in the same positions.
LinearAlgebra.a row canonical matrix?: A matrix A is said to be in row canonical form if it satisfies the following properties:       (1) All zero rows, if any, are at the bottom of the matrix.       (2) Each leading nonzero entry in a row is to the right of the leading nonzero entry in the preceding row.       (3) Each pivot (leading nonzero entry) is equal to 1.       (4) Each pivot is the only nonzero entry in its column.
LinearAlgebra.the row spaces of row canonical matrice: Suppose A and B are row canonical matrices. Then A and B have the same row space if and only if they have the same nonzero rows.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between matrices and r: Every matrix A is row equivalent to a unique matrix in row canonical form.
LinearAlgebra.a homogeneous system of linear equation: A homogeneous linear system with more unknowns than equations has a nontrivial solution.
LinearAlgebra.a linearly dependent list?: We say that the vectors v_1, ..., v_m in V are linearly dependent if there exist scalars a_1, ..., a_m in K, not all 0 such that       a_1v_1 + ... + a_mv_m = 0.
LinearAlgebra.a linearly independent list?: A list is called linearly independent if whenever a member of this list can be expressed as a linear combination of the other vectors, then each coefficient is 0.
LinearAlgebra.a list?: A list in a vector space V is an ordered set v_1, ..., v_n of vectors in V.
LinearAlgebra.Is a list that contains 0 linearly indep: Suppose 0 is a vector in v_1, ..., v_n. Then these vectors are linearly dependent.
LinearAlgebra.Is a list of length 1 linearly independe: Suppose v is a nonzero vector. Then v, by itself, is linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.subsets of independent sets of vectors?: If a set S of vectors is linearly independent, then any subset of S is also linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between linear combina: Suppose two or more nonzero vectors v_1, ..., v_m are linearly dependent. Then one of the vectors is a linear combination of the preceding vectors.
LinearAlgebra.echilon matrix relate to linear independ: The nonzero rows of a matrix in echilon form are linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.a k-linear combination?: Let V be a vector space over a field K. A vector v in V is a K-linear combination of vectors u_1, u_2, ..., u_m in V if there exist scalars a_1, a_2, ..., a_m in K such that v = a_1u_1 + a_2u_2 + ... + a_mu_m.
LinearAlgebra.the set of k-linear combinations spanne: The collection of all linear combinations of u_1, ..., u_m is called the linear span of u_1, ..., u_m, denoted span(u_1, ..., u_m) or <u_1, ..., u_m>
LinearAlgebra.the intersection of all subspaces of a: The intersection of all subspaces containing a list X is the span of the list X.
LinearAlgebra.a finite dimensional vector space?: A vector space V is called finite dimensional if it is spanned by a finite list.
LinearAlgebra.an infinite dimensional vector space?: A vector space V is called infinite dimensional if it is not spanned by any finite list.
LinearAlgebra.an n-dimensional vector space?: A n dimensional vector space is a vector space whose basis contains exactly n elements.
LinearAlgebra.What 2 statements can we conclude from a: If V is a vector space, then the following conditions on a list X = v_1, ..., v_m spanning V are equivalent:       1. X is not the shortest spanning list.       2. Some v_i //in X is in the subspace spanned by the others.       3. There is a v_i //in X that is a linear combination of some other elements of X.
LinearAlgebra.a member of X being spanned by other mem: If V is a vector space, then the following conditions on a list X = v_1, ..., v_m spanning V are equivalent:       1. X is not the shortest spanning list.       2. Some v_i //in X is in the subspace spanned by the others.       3. There is a v_i //in X that is a linear combination of some other elements of X.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between shortest spann: If X = v_1, ..., v_m is a list spanning a vector space V, then X is a shortest spanning list if and only if X is linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.a basis for a vector space?: A basis for a vector space is a linearly independent list that spans V.
LinearAlgebra.the standard basis for a field?: If our vector space is K^n, then the basis X = {e_1, ..., e_n} is called the standard basis of K^n
LinearAlgebra.Suppose V is an n-dimensional vector spa: Let V be an n-dimensional vector space over a field K. Then V K^n.
LinearAlgebra.the coordinate set of a vector?: Every vector in a vector space can be expressed in a unique way by the basis. The coordinate set is the set of coordinates used to express a given vector using a known basis.
LinearAlgebra.finite dimensional vector spaces relate: Every finite-dimensional vector space V has a basis.
LinearAlgebra.a list with more elements than the basi: If a list has more elements than the basis, then this is a linearly dependent list.
LinearAlgebra.a linearly independent list of size n i: Suppose V is a vector space with dimension n. Then any linearly independent set S = {u_1, ..., u_n} with n elements is a basis of V.
LinearAlgebra.about a spanning set of size n in an n: Suppose V is a vector space with dimension n. Then any spanning set S = {u_1, ..., u_n} with n elements is a basis of V.
LinearAlgebra.spanning set for the Vector Space V, how: Any maximum number of linearly independent vectors in S form a basis of V.
LinearAlgebra.the largest size a linearly independent: The largest size a linearly independent list can have in a vector space of dimension n is of size n.
LinearAlgebra.a vector space with two bases?: If X and Y are bases of a vector space then they have the same number of elements.
LinearAlgebra.length of 2 different bases for a vector: If X and Y are bases of a vector space then they have the same number of elements.
LinearAlgebra.the dimension of a vector space?: The dimension of a vector space is the number of elements in its basis.
LinearAlgebra.the rank of a matrix?: The rank of a matrix A, written rank(A), is equal to the maximum number of linearly independent rows of A,       or equivalently the dimension of the rowspace of A.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the dimension: The dimension of the rowspace of a matrix A is equal to the dimension of the columnspace of A.
Suppose U and W are finite dimensional subspaces of a vector space V. Then       dim(U+W) = dim U + dim W - dim(U \cap W)
LinearAlgebra.the direct sum of subspaces?: A vector space V is the direct sum of subspaces W_1, ..., W_r, written       V = W_1 \oplus ... \oplus W_r       if every vector v //in V can be written uniquely in the form v = w_1 + ... + w_r, with w_i //in W_i.
LinearAlgebra.If V s the direct sum of W_1, ..., W_r,: Suppose V = W_1 \oplus ... \oplus W_r. Then       dim V = dim W_1 + ... + dim W_r.
LinearAlgebra.If V s the direct sum of W_1, ..., W_r a: Suppose V = W_1 \oplus ... \oplus W_r. If S_i is a basis for W_i, then \cap>_kS_k is a basis for V.
LinearAlgebra.a diagonalizable matrix?: Suppose A is an n-square matrix. The matrix A is said to be diagonalizable if there exists a nonsingular matrix P such that B = P^{-1}AP is diagonal.
LinearAlgebra.a linear mapping?: Let V and U be vector spaces over the same field K. A mapping F: V \to U is called a linear mapping or linear transformation if it satisfies the following two conditions       1. For any vectors v, w //in V, F(v + w) = F(v) + F(w),       2. For any scalar k and vector v //in V, F(kv) = kF(v).
LinearAlgebra.a linear transformation?: Let V and U be vector spaces over the same field K. A mapping F: V \to U is called a linear mapping or linear transformation if it satisfies the following two conditions       1. For any vectors v, w //in V, F(v + w) = F(v) + F(w),       2. For any scalar k and vector v //in V, F(kv) = kF(v).
LinearAlgebra.Suppose U and V are vector spaces over K: Suppose U and V are vector spaces over a field K. If {v_1, ..., v_n} is a basis for V and u_1, ..., u_n are vectors in U. Then there is a unique linear mapping F, such that F(v_i) = u_i.
LinearAlgebra.isomorphic vector spaces?: Two vector spaces are isomorphic if there is a bijective linear mapping between them.
LinearAlgebra.the kernel of a linear mapping?: Let F: V \to U be a linear mapping. Then the kernel of F is the set of vectors in V that map into the zero vector in U.
LinearAlgebra.the image of a linear mapping?: Let F: V \to U be a linear mapping. Then the image of F is the set of vectors in U that are mapped to.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the kernel of: Let F: V \to U be a linear mapping. Then the kernel of F is a subspace of V.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the image of a: Let F: V \to U be a linear mapping. Then the image of F is a subspace of U.
Suppose F: V \to U is a linear mapping. Suppose v_1, ..., v_n span V. Then F(v_1), ..., F(v_n) span Im F.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between an arbitrary m: Let A be any m \times n matrix over a field K viewed as a linear map A: K^n \to K<sup>m</sup>.
view the matrix A as a linear mapping. Then Ker A = NullSp(A).
view the matrix A as a linear mapping. Then Im A = ColSp(A).
LinearAlgebra.the rank of a linear mapping?: The rank of a linear mapping is the dimension of its image.
LinearAlgebra.the nullity of a linear mapping?: The nullity of a linear mapping is the dimension of its kernel.
LinearAlgebra.Suppose F: V \\to U is a linear mapping,: Suppose F: V \to U is a linear mapping, where V is a finite dimensional vector space. Then       dim V = dim(Ker F) + dim(Im F)
LinearAlgebra.a homogeneous linear system?: If K is a field, then a homogeneous linear system over K of m equations in n unknowns is an equation AX = 0, where A is an m \times n matrix, X is a vector of n elements, and 0 is the zero vector.
LinearAlgebra.a solution to a homogeneous linear syst: A solution to a homogeneous linear system is a vector u = (c_1, ..., c_n) //in K^n, where Au = 0.
LinearAlgebra.a nontrivial solution to a homogeneous: A solution to a homogeneous linear system is nontrivial if it is a nonzero solution.
LinearAlgebra.the solution set of a homogeneous syste: The solution set W of a homogeneous system AX = 0 in n unknowns is a subspace of K^n.
LinearAlgebra.the solution space?: The set of all solutions to a homogeneous linear system forms a subspace of k^n of the system, called the solution space
LinearAlgebra.the formula for the dimension of the so: The dimension of the solution space of a system of linear equations is             s = n - r       where s is the dimension of the solution space, n is the number of unknowns, and r is the rank of the coefficient matrix A.
LinearAlgebra.a singular linear mapping?: A linear operator is said to be singular if there is some nonzero vector that maps to zero.
LinearAlgebra.a nonsingular linear mapping?: A linear operator is said to be nonsingular if there is no nonzero vector that maps to zero.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between nonsingular ma: Let F: V \to U be a nonsingular linear mapping. Then the image of any linearly independent set is linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between injective mapp: A linear mapping is injective if and only if it is nonsingular.
LinearAlgebra.the collection of all linear mappings f: The collection of all linear mappings from V into U is called Hom(V, U) and it is a vector space over K. .
LinearAlgebra.the dimension of Hom(U, V)?: If dim U = m and dim V = n, then dim(Hom(U, V)) = mn
LinearAlgebra.a diagonalizable linear operator?: Suppose T is a linear operator. The linear operator T is said to be diagonalizable if there exists a basis S of V such that the matrix representation of T relative to the basis S is a diagonal matrix D.
LinearAlgebra.How do polynomials behave over the linea: Let f and g be polynomials. For any square matrix A and scalar k,       1. (f + g)(A) = f(A) = g(A)       2. fg(A) = f(A)g(A)       3. (kf)(A) = kf(A)       4. f(A)g(A) = g(A)f(A).
LinearAlgebra.the powers of a linear transformation T: Powers of T are defined by the composition operation, that is       T^2 = T \composition T, ... etc.
LinearAlgebra.the characteristic polynomial of A?: \cdelta(t) - det(tI_n - A) is a polynomial in t of degree n and is called the characteristic polynomial of A.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the characteri: A matrix A and its transpose A^T have the same characteristic polynomial.
LinearAlgebra.the Caley-Hamiltonian theorem for matri: The Caley-Hamiltonian theorem says that Every matrix is a root of its characteristic polynomial.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between a matrix and i: The Caley-Hamiltonian theorem says that Every matrix is a root of its characteristic polynomial.
LinearAlgebra.similar matrices?: A and B are similar if there exists an invertible P such that B = P^{-1}AP.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between characteristic: Similar matrixes have the same characteristic polynomial
LinearAlgebra.the Caley-Hamiltonian theorem for linea: The Caley-Hamiltonian theorem says that every linear operator is a zero of its characteristic polynomial.
LinearAlgebra.an eigenvalue of A?: Let A be any square matrix. A scalar \lambda is called an eigenvalue of A if there exists a nonzero (column) vector v such that Av = \lambdav.
LinearAlgebra.an eigenvector of A?: Any vector that satisfies the relation Av = \lambdav for some \lambda is an eigenvector of A.
LinearAlgebra.A being similar to a diagonal matrix?: An n-square matrix A is similar to a diagonal matrix D if and only if A has n linearly independent eigenvectors. In this case the diagonal elements of D are the corresponding eigenvalues and D = P^{-1}AP, where P is the matrix whose columns are the eigenvectors.
LinearAlgebra.A having dimension n and having n linear: An n-square matrix A is similar to a diagonal matrix D if and only if A has n linearly independent eigenvectors. In this case the diagonal elements of D are the corresponding eigenvalues and D = P^{-1}AP, where P is the matrix whose columns are the eigenvectors.
LinearAlgebra.0 an eigenvalue of A?: The scalar 0 is an eigenvalue of A if and only if A is singular (non invertible).
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between eigenvalues of: If \lambda is an eigenvalue of A, where A is invertible, then \lambda^{-1} is an eigenvalue of A^{-1}.
LinearAlgebra.What two statements can we conclude from: Let A be a square matrix. Then the following are equivalent.       1. A scalar \lambda is an eigenvalue of A.       2. The matrix M = A - \lambdaI is singular.       3. The scalar \lambda is a root of the characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) of A.

Let A be a square matrix. Then the following are equivalent.       1. A scalar \lambda is an eigenvalue of A.       2. The matrix M = A - \lambdaI is singular.       3. The scalar \lambda is a root of the characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) of A.

Let A be a square matrix. Then the following are equivalent.       1. A scalar \lambda is an eigenvalue of A.       2. The matrix M = A - \lambdaI is singular.       3. The scalar \lambda is a root of the characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) of A.
LinearAlgebra.square matrices over \\mathbb{C}?: Let A be a square matrix over \mathbb{C}. Then A has at least one eigenvalue.
LinearAlgebra.distinct eigenvectors each representing: Suppose v_1, ..., v_n are nonzero eigenvectors of a matrix A belonging to the distinct eigenvalues \lambda_1, ..., \lambda_n. Then v_1, ..., v_n are linearly independent.
LinearAlgebra.the characteristic polynomial of a n-squ: Suppose the characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) of an n-square matrix A is a product of n distinct factors,       say \cdelta(t) = (t - a_1)...(t - a_n). Then A is similar to the diagonal matrix D = diag(a_1, ..., a_n).
LinearAlgebra.the algebraic multiplicity of \\lambda?: If \lambda is an eigenvalue of a matrix A, then the algebraic multiplicity of \lambda is defined to be the multiplicity of \lambda as a root of the characteristic polynomial of A.
LinearAlgebra.the geometric multiplicity of \\lambda?: If \lambda is an eigenvalue of a matrix A, then the geometric multiplicity of \lambda is defined to be the dimension of the eigenspace of \lambda.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between algebraic mult: The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue \lambda of a matrix A does not exceed its algebraic multiplicity.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between diagonalizable: Suppose A is a matrix representation of T. Then T is diagonalizable if and only if A is diagonalizable.
LinearAlgebra.the eigenvalues of a real symmetric mat: Let A be a real symmetric matrix. Then each root \lambda of its characteristic polynomial is real.
LinearAlgebra.distinct eigenvectors of a real symmetr: Let A be a real symmetric matrix. Suppose u and v are eigenvectors of A belonging to distinct eigenvalues \lambda_1 and \lambda_2. Then u and v are orthogonal, that is, <u, v> = 0.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between real symmetric: Let A be a real symmetric matrix. Then there exists an orthogonal matrix P such that D = P^{-1}AP is diagonal.
LinearAlgebra.a monic polynomial?: A polynomial is monic if its leading coefficient equals one.
LinearAlgebra.a minimum polynomial?: The minimum polynomial, m(t) of the matrix A is the monic polynomial of lowest degree such that m(A) = 0.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the minimum po: The minimum polynomial m(t) of a matrix A divides every polynomial that has A as a root. In particular, m(t) divides the characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) of A.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the irreducibl: The characteristic polynomial \cdelta(t) and the minimum polynomial m(t) of a matrix A have the same irreducible factors.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between eigenvalues of: A scalar, \lambda, is an eigenvalue of the matrix A if and only if \lambda is a root of the minimum polynomial A.
LinearAlgebra.an invariant subspace of T?: Let T: V \to V be linear. A subspace W of V is said to be invariant under T or T-invariant if T maps W into itself. In this case T|_W defines a linear operator on W.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between invariant subs: Let T: V \to V be any linear operator, and let f(t) be any polynomial. Then the kernel of f(f) is invariant under T.
LinearAlgebra.a block matrix?: matrix A, M is a block matrix of A if M is a partition of A into submatrices called blocks.
LinearAlgebra.a square block matrix?: Let M be a matrix. Then M is called a square block matrix if:       1. M is a square matrix.       2. The blocks form a square matrix.       3. The diagonal blocks are also square matrices.
LinearAlgebra.the block diagonal matrix of a linear o:
Suppose W is an invariant subspace of T: V \to V. Then T has a block matrix representation
AB
0C
, where A is the matrix representation of the restriction of T to W.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the basis of a: Suppose W_1, W_2, ..., W_r are subspaces of V and suppose B_1 = {w<sub>11</sub>, ..., w_{1n_1}}, ... B_r = {w_{r1}, ..., w_{rn_r}} are bases of W_1, ..., W_r respectively.       Then V is the direct sum of W_i if and only if the union B = B_1 \cap> ... \cap> B_r is a basis of V.
LinearAlgebra.the block diagonal matrix of a linear o: Suppose T: V \to V is linear and suppose V is the direct sum of T-invariant subspaces, say W_1, ..., W_r. If A_i is a matrix representation of the restriction of T to W_i, then T can be represented by the block diagonal matrix       M = diag(A_1, ..., A_r).
LinearAlgebra.Jordan Canonical Form?: Let T: V \to V be a linear operator. Then T has a block diagonal matrix representation, called its Jordan Canonical Form (JCF) J in which each diagonal entry is a Jordan block J<sub>ij</sub> = J(\lambda_i).
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the minimum po: If m(t) = (t - \lambda_1)^{m_1}...(t - \lambda_r)<sup>m_r</sup>, where the \lambda_i are distinct scalars.      Then the JCF of T has a Jordan block of size m_i for each i between 1 and r.
LinearAlgebra.the relationship between the sum of the: If \cdelta(t) = (t - \lambda_1)<sup>n_1</sup>...(t - \lambda_r)^{n_r}, where the \lambda_i are distinct scalars.      Then the sum of the dimensions of the Jordan blocks for a given \lambda_i equals n_i.
LinearAlgebra.How many Jordan Blocks will there be for: The number of Jordan blocks for a given \lambda is equal to the geometric multiplicity of \lambda.
LinearAlgebra.the T-cyclic subspace of V generated by: If T is a linear operator on V, and v //in V (v \ne 0), then the set of all vectors of the form f(T)(v), where f(t) ranges over all polynomials over K, is the T-invariant subspace of V called the T-cyclic subspace of V generated by v.
LinearAlgebra.the T-annihilator of v and Z(v, T)?: Consider the sequence v, T(v), T^2(v), ... of powers of T acting on v. Let k be the least integer such that T^k(v) is a linear combination of those vectors that precede it in this sequence.      say, T^k(v) = -a_{k-1}T^{k-1}(v) - ... - a_0v.      Then m_v(t) is the unique monic polynomial of lowest degree for which m_v(T)(v) = 0.      We call m_v(t) the T-annihilator of v and Z(v, T).
LinearAlgebra.a basis for the set Z(v, T)?: The set {v, T(v), ..., T^{k-1}(v)} is a basis of Z(v, T); hence dim Z(v, T) = k.
LinearAlgebra.the representation T_v represent?: T_v represents the restriction of T to Z(v, T).
LinearAlgebra.the minimum polynomial of T_v?: The minimum polynomial of T_v is m_v(t).
